title: Hidden Prime-Factor Subgroups in Molecular and Condensed-Phase Systems
abstract: We describe a group theoretic analysis of Shor's algorithm and other related hidden subgroup problems in mathematics and relate these to symmetries of molecular and condensed phase assemblies. By recasting Shor's algorithm through the lens of group theory, we expose the possibility that physical systems such as molecular orbitals, condensed phase assemblies and optical beams may be designed such that these contain information pertaining to the solution to hard mathematical problems such as prime-factoring. We discuss real molecular systems, whose orbitals are constructed from symmetry-adapted linear combinations of atomic orbitals, and show that these contain information pertaining to the prime-factors of corresponding integers. Due to the broad significance of prime-factoring towards a variety of encryption problems in cyber-security, we believe this novel and fundamental approach may have broad impact.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the mathematical structure of Shor's algorithm can be mapped onto the physical symmetries of molecules and periodic lattices. By showing that prime factors are encoded in the Fourier modes of these systems, it suggests that engineered matter could potentially be used to solve hard mathematical problems.
main_problem: Investigating whether the group-theoretic structure of Shor's algorithm and the Hidden Subgroup Problem (HSP) can be physically realized using the symmetries of molecular and condensed-phase systems.
main_result: The authors establish a formal mathematical mapping between the modular arithmetic used in Shor's algorithm and the periodic symmetries found in molecular orbitals and Bloch wavefunctions, suggesting that physical systems could potentially encode prime-factor information.
method: The study uses group theory, specifically representation theory, the Chinese Remainder Theorem, and projection operators, to relate cyclic group rotations to symmetry-adapted linear combinations (SALCs) and Bloch's theorem.
model_or_system: The models include molecular systems (e.g., hexahydroxybenzene, polyenes), condensed-phase assemblies (periodic lattices, quantum corrals), and optical beams with specific angular momenta, all modeled via rotation groups and periodic potentials.
key_observables: The Fourier structure of molecular orbitals, Bloch phases, and the period 'r' of the modular exponentiation function.
important_parameters: The integer N to be factored, its prime factors b_i, and the subdivision factor 'a' used to define the extended group G_{N,a}.
main_assumptions: The experimental realization of these engineered systems remains an open question; the model assumes electrons in polyene systems exist in a periodic 1D potential extending to the required residues.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate quantum circuit templates, the step-by-step correspondence between Shor's algorithm and physical symmetries, visual representations of group rotations and molecular structures (like hexahydroxybenzene), and the division of groups into cosets.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining modular arithmetic as a rotation group, develops the representation theory for these groups, formulates the HSP, and then establishes the structural correspondence to molecular orbitals and Bloch wavefunctions.
why_interesting: It proposes a novel bridge between abstract quantum algorithms and tangible physical symmetries in matter, suggesting that synthetic molecular or lattice engineering could serve as a platform for physicalized quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2: Step-by-step correspondence between Shor’s algorithm (left), the construction of symmetry-adapted molec- ular orbitals (center-right), and Bloch-wave / optical systems (far right). The shared mathematical structure at each step (center) is an operational identity. Speculative steps—whose experimental realization remains open—are shown with pale fills and dashed borders (†).

FIG. 3: Illustration of (a): G6, The circle is divided into N equal portions and the colors are chosen purely to make the regions distinct. The isomorphic objects being constructed here are symmetric with rotations, (Figure (a) was generated using the AI tool Microsoft Copilot.) Figure (b) is the molecule hexabydroxyben- zene (or benzenehexol), with the OH groups pointing out of the plane, that has the same symmetry as G6.

FIG. 4: Illustration of (a): G15,2, (b): G21,10 and (c): G21,2. In Figure (c) we have chose a = 2, unlike in Eq. (28). In each figure, the circle is first divided into N equal portions and then each slice is further subdi- vided in a parts. The colors are chosen purely to make the regions distinct. The isomorphic objects being con- structed here are symmetric with rotations, and in Sec- tions IV B and V we discuss functions defined on these objects. Also see Figure 5. (These images were gener- ated using the AI tool Microsoft Copilot.)

FIG. 5: Similar to Figure 4, but figures (a) and (b) are G6,2 and G6,3, respectively. Figure (c) is the molecule hexabydroxybenzene (or benzenehexol), with the OH groups pointing out of the plane, that has the same symmetry as G6, which, as shown in Sections IV B is a hidden subgroup of G6,2 and G6,3. (Figures (a) and (b) were generated using the AI tool Microsoft Copilot.)

FIG. 6: Illustration of (a): Eq. (32) and (b): Eq. (36) for the group G15,2. In both cases, the cosets generated by Ci aNGN (Figure (a)) and Ci aNGa (Figure (b)) are depicted using the same color. For example, in Figure (a), the action of all Ci+ja aN ; ∀j = [0, N −1], on GN, for odd or even values of i yields either the black or the white regions. Likewise in Figure (b) the action of all Ci+jN aN ; ∀j = [0, a −1], on Ga, yields each region with a specific color. As we show here, functions such as f(x) = ax (mod N) (also see Section VI) have the same values inside the, thus created cosets, and hence hide GN. (These images were generated using the AI tool Microsoft Copilot.)
